sourceTitle: Notion for admins of large teams

questions.question: Which Notion plan unlocks the admin‑only features described in this guide?

options.text: Free
options.explanation: Free plans do not include admin controls.

options.text: Personal
options.explanation: Personal plans are for individual use, not admin features.

options.text: Team
options.explanation: Team plans give collaboration tools but not the full admin suite.

options.text: Enterprise
options.explanation: Enterprise plans provide the admin‑only capabilities highlighted in the article.

questions.question: Where can an admin add new people to a workspace or change existing members’ access levels?

options.text: In the sidebar’s quick‑add menu
options.explanation: The sidebar only shows navigation, not member management.

options.text: In the Members section of Settings
options.explanation: The article states that admins manage members from the Members section of Settings.

options.text: On the Notion homepage dashboard
options.explanation: The homepage does not contain member‑management controls.

options.text: Via the template gallery
options.explanation: Templates are for page content, not for adding users.

questions.question: How can an admin automatically allow anyone with a specific email domain to join the workspace?

options.text: By sending a manual invitation to each user
options.explanation: Manual invitations are one‑by‑one, not automatic.

options.text: By adding the domain to the workspace’s allowlist in Settings
options.explanation: Typing the domain in Settings lets anyone with that domain join automatically.

options.text: By enabling public sharing for the workspace
options.explanation: Public sharing makes pages viewable, not a way to auto‑add members.

options.text: By creating a group named after the domain
options.explanation: Groups organize members after they’re added; they don’t auto‑invite users.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of setting up SCIM in a Notion workspace?

options.text: To customize the look of the workspace sidebar
options.explanation: SCIM does not affect UI appearance.

options.text: To manage provisioning of users and permission groups automatically
options.explanation: SCIM is a standard for automated user and group provisioning, as noted in the article.

options.text: To enable video playback without an ad blocker
options.explanation: Video playback is unrelated to SCIM.

options.text: To create public URLs for Notion pages
options.explanation: Public URLs are set via domain settings, not SCIM.

questions.question: Where can an admin manually create groups for organizing members?

options.text: In the Group tab of the Members section
options.explanation: The guide specifies groups are created from the Group tab under Members.

options.text: In the Workspace Templates gallery
options.explanation: Templates are for page content, not group management.

options.text: On the Notion mobile app settings page
options.explanation: Group creation is done in the desktop/web Settings, not specifically the mobile app.

options.text: Via the third‑party integrations panel
options.explanation: Integrations control apps, not internal member groups.

questions.question: Which security measure can an admin enable to stop pages from being moved or exported?

options.text: Turn on version history for all pages
options.explanation: Version history tracks changes but does not block moving or exporting.

options.text: Enable page lock on each page individually
options.explanation: Page lock restricts editing, not moving or exporting at the workspace level.

options.text: Activate security settings that limit sidebar changes and prevent pages from being moved or exported
options.explanation: The article lists this as a specific admin security option.

options.text: Disable all third‑party integrations
options.explanation: Disabling integrations affects external apps, not page movement or export controls.

questions.question: How does an admin control which third‑party integrations are allowed in the workspace?

options.text: By setting permissions in the Members section
options.explanation: Member permissions govern access, not integration allowances.

options.text: Through the workspace’s integration settings, enabling or disabling each app
options.explanation: Admins manage allowed integrations directly in workspace settings, as described.

options.text: By adding a custom domain for shared pages
options.explanation: Domain settings affect URLs, not integration permissions.

options.text: By creating a separate group for each integration
options.explanation: Groups organize users, not integration access.

questions.question: What recent change to the sidebar is highlighted in the guide to make sharing with the right groups easier?

options.text: A new ‘Share with Teamspaces’ button that auto‑assigns groups
options.explanation: The guide mentions teamspaces but not a specific button.

options.text: Updated sidebar elements that integrate with teamspaces, giving each team a dedicated home for work
options.explanation: The article notes that sidebar updates and teamspaces simplify sharing with appropriate groups.

options.text: A collapsible sidebar that hides all sharing options by default
options.explanation: Hiding sharing options would not make sharing easier.

options.text: Removal of the ‘Members’ section from the sidebar
options.explanation: The Members section remains; it is not removed.
